skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents through inference.sh. It helps agents open pages, inspect element refs, interact with forms, capture screenshots, record sessions, use proxies, upload files, and run page JavaScript.
skill.summary: Automate browser sessions with element refs, screenshots, video, proxies, and page JavaScript.
skill.author: inferencesh

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Most command and path findings are documentation examples or shell templates, but the skill legitimately enables browser automation, proxy routing, JavaScript execution, file uploads, screenshots, videos, and authenticated workflows. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious destination was found, so this is publishable only with a clear sensitive-data warning.

high_findings.title: Cookie Extraction Guidance Can Expose Session Secrets
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The authentication guide demonstrates extracting document.cookie from an authenticated browser session and echoing the result. This can expose session identifiers or other sensitive cookies in logs or downstream tools. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines directly show cookie extraction and printing; the risk is sensitive-data exposure, although no malicious external destination is shown.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show cookie extraction and printing. The risk is sensitive-data exposure, although no malicious external destination is shown.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The quick start tells users to pipe a remote installer script into sh before logging in. This is a known supply-chain risk because the fetched script executes with the user privileges of the shell. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The pattern is explicit and executable, but the URL appears to be the vendor domain used by the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and executable. The confidence is below very high because it points to the expected vendor CLI domain, not an obviously malicious host.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Browser Automation Surface
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill allows infsh commands, browser interactions, screenshots, file upload, proxy configuration, and JavaScript execution. These are legitimate browser automation features but can affect authenticated sessions or sensitive pages if misused. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: Multiple cited lines document the exact capabilities; the remaining uncertainty is user intent during use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple cited lines document the exact capabilities. The remaining uncertainty is whether a user applies the capability to sensitive or unauthorized targets.

low_findings.title: Most Markdown Command and Path Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many external command, path traversal, and weak cryptography flags are caused by Markdown code fences, tables, refs, placeholders, or relative documentation links. They do not execute by themselves when the skill is installed. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: Representative lines show documentation syntax rather than active hidden code, but shell templates remain real runnable examples covered by other findings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative lines show documentation syntax rather than active hidden code. Shell templates remain runnable examples and are covered separately.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE CHECK: No evidence found for text that claims reviewer authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or tries to override the audit process. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: A targeted semantic check found only ordinary documentation language, but this does not prove every future revision is free of injection text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted semantic check found only ordinary documentation language. This does not prove every future revision is free of injection text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Install Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start uses a remote curl response piped directly into sh. This should be replaced or supplemented with checksum and package-manager installation guidance. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The exact executable pattern is present on the cited line.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact executable pattern is present on the cited line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Cookie Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide extracts and prints cookies from the active page. This can leak session data through terminal history, logs, or copied outputs. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines extract document.cookie, parse the result, and echo it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines extract document.cookie, parse the result, and echo it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function runs arbitrary JavaScript in the current page context. This is useful for inspection but can read page data and trigger page-side behavior. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The function and input field are explicitly documented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The function and input field are explicitly documented.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Routing for Browser Traffic
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill supports HTTP and SOCKS proxy configuration. This is valid for testing but can obscure browsing origin or send traffic through untrusted infrastructure. Confidence: 0.80. Confidence reasoning: Proxy and SOCKS examples are directly documented in the cited lines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Proxy and SOCKS examples are directly documented in the cited lines.

content.value_statement: Manual browser testing and data collection can be slow and hard to repeat. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured browser actions through inference.sh sessions.
actual_capabilities: Opens web pages and returns interactive element references for later actions.
Clicks, fills, types, selects, hovers, drags, scrolls, uploads files, and navigates browser history.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots from active browser sessions.
Records browser sessions and returns the video when the session closes.
Runs JavaScript in the current page context for extraction or inspection.
Supports proxy configuration, custom viewport size, custom user agent, and authenticated workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Element references can expire after navigation or dynamic page changes.
Actions depend on site permissions, anti-bot controls, and page stability.
Sensitive sessions, cookies, screenshots, and recordings need careful handling.

use_cases.title: Automate QA Browser Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable login, form, navigation, screenshot, and recording workflows for web application checks.

use_cases.title: Collect Web Research Evidence
use_cases.description: Open pages, capture visible evidence, extract page text, and save screenshots for later review.

use_cases.title: Prototype Agent Web Workflows
use_cases.description: Test how an agent can browse, inspect elements, interact with pages, and recover from stale references.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to open {url}. Summarize the page title, visible interactive elements, and the safest next action.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Full Page Screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Open {url}, take a full-page screenshot, and report any visible page errors or missing content.

prompt_templates.title: Fill and Submit a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Open {form_url}, identify the current element refs, fill the fields with {test_data}, submit the form, then re-snapshot the result.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Authenticated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an agent-browser session for {login_url}, complete the approved login steps, navigate to {target_page}, capture a screenshot, and close the session.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and inspect available actions.
output_examples.output: The response lists the page title, current URL, visible buttons and links with element refs, and a screenshot attachment.

output_examples.input: Record a short checkout test with safe test data.
output_examples.output: The response describes each action, reports the final page state, includes screenshots, and returns a video after closing the session.

output_examples.input: Extract page headings and links from a documentation page.
output_examples.output: The response summarizes the extracted headings, important links, and any navigation problems found during the session.

best_practices: Use test accounts and non-sensitive data whenever possible.
Take a fresh snapshot after navigation, form submission, or dynamic content changes.
Close sessions after work is complete to limit retained browser state and recordings.
anti_patterns: Do not automate sites without permission or against their terms of service.
Do not print, store, or share cookies, passwords, tokens, screenshots, or recordings unnecessarily.
Do not reuse stale element refs after page navigation or major DOM changes.

faq.question: Does this skill control a real browser?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses inference.sh browser sessions with Playwright-style actions and element references.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require local setup?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need the inference.sh CLI, login, and access to the agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it handle authenticated pages?
faq.answer: Yes, but users should use test accounts and avoid exposing passwords, cookies, or tokens.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes. The execute function runs JavaScript in the current page context.

faq.question: What should users do when an element ref fails?
faq.answer: They should run a fresh snapshot and use the new refs returned by the page.
